Description: Clear up the most common misconception upfront — that 'internet' and 'web' are the same thing. Use the highway-vs-shops analogy: the internet is the global road network of cables and satellites; the WWW is all the websites (shops) built on top of it. End with the course hook: this course is about building those shops.
Learning Outcome: Student can confidently explain the difference between the internet and the WWW using a real-world analogy.
Activity: Quick-fire tag: 5 everyday scenarios (opening a webpage, sending email, WhatsApp call, Google search, FTP upload) — student labels each as 'Internet' or 'WWW' and explains why.

Topic: Websites as the Building Blocks of the WWW
Description: Zoom into what a website actually is: a collection of files (HTML, CSS, JS) sitting on a computer somewhere, served to anyone who asks. Tease the three-layer model — structure, style, behaviour — without going deep yet. Close with: every site you have ever used was built with exactly these three things.
Learning Outcome: Student understands that a website = files on a server, and knows the course will teach them to build those files.
Activity: Web Archaeology: compare a well-known site (Amazon or Wikipedia) in 2005 vs today using the Wayback Machine. Note 3 structural or visual changes — no coding, just observation.

Topic: Chapter 2: You Click a Link : What Happens?
The Journey Begins — Client Side (Visual / Story-driven)
Description: Story-first, visual reading. Follow a single click from the moment the user hits Enter: browser parses the URL, checks its cache, asks the OS to resolve the address, and hands the request to the network. Intentionally light on jargon — the goal is a strong intuitive mental model before the technical detail in the next topic.
Learning Outcome: Student can narrate the client-side journey in plain English, step by step, without needing technical terms.
Activity: Request Detective Pt 1: open DevTools → Network tab on any site. Find the very first request. Screenshot and label: URL, method, status code. No explanation needed yet — just observation.

Topic: Under the Hood — URLs, DNS & the Handshake
Description: Now go technical. Dissect a full URL segment by segment: scheme, domain, path, query string, fragment. Explain DNS as the internet's phonebook — translating a domain name into an IP address. Introduce the TCP handshake at a conceptual level (three steps, no packet depth). End: the request is now travelling toward a server.
Learning Outcome: Student can break any URL into its named parts and explain what DNS does in one sentence.
Activity: URL Dissector: 5 URLs of varying complexity (subdomain, query string, hash, port, path). Student labels every segment. Bonus: look up the DNS record for a domain using a public tool like nslookup.io.

Topic: Chapter 3: The Server Receives Your Request
What Is a Server? — Concept & Analogy
Description: Demystify the server: it is just a computer, always on, waiting for requests. Use the restaurant analogy — client = diner, server = kitchen, database = pantry. Walk through the server's decision loop: receive request → determine what is needed → fetch from DB if required → prepare and send response. Introduce HTTP methods intuitively: GET = asking for something, POST = sending something.
Learning Outcome: Student can describe what a server does using the restaurant analogy and distinguish GET from POST intuitively.
Activity: Role Play: given 5 user actions (load homepage, submit login, search for a product, upload a photo, log out), student labels each GET or POST and writes one sentence on what the server probably does.

Description: Introduce HTTP as the agreed language client and server use to talk. Show the structure of an HTTP response: status line, headers, body. Make status codes memorable with real examples: 200 OK, 301 Moved, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error. Annotate a raw HTTP response visually — every part labelled. Close: the server has packaged HTML, CSS, and JS files and sent them back.
Learning Outcome: Student can read a basic HTTP response, identify its parts, and know what the most common status codes mean.
Activity: Request Detective Pt 2 (DevTools): inspect 5 requests across 2 different sites. Log method, status code, content-type, and response time for each. Find one redirect and one failed request.

Topic: Chapter 4: The Browser Builds the Page
From Files to Pixels — the Rendering Pipeline
Description: Walk through what the browser does once it has the files: parse HTML → build DOM → parse CSS → build CSSOM → merge into Render Tree → Layout → Paint. Keep it conceptual, no code. Use a pipeline diagram. Land the mental model: HTML is the skeleton, CSS is the skin, JS is the muscles.
Learning Outcome: Student understands the browser's rendering pipeline conceptually and knows what the DOM is.
Activity: DOM Explorer: open DevTools → Elements tab on any site. Find 10 elements, note the tag name and 2 CSS properties for each. Mark which are block-level and which are inline. No coding.

Topic: The Browser as a Platform & Intro to DevTools
Description: Reframe the browser: not just a viewer but a full runtime environment that executes JS, manages storage, and handles events. Quick tour of the three most useful DevTools tabs: Elements, Console, Network. Module close-out: recap the complete journey from URL typed to page painted. Teaser: everything you see in a browser comes from HTML, CSS, and JS — and that is exactly what you will build next.
Learning Outcome: Student can use Elements and Console tabs in DevTools with confidence and can narrate the full request-to-render journey end to end.
Activity: Full Journey Map: student draws the complete request-response-render cycle (on paper or Excalidraw), labelling every stage covered across the module. This is the module capstone — can be used as a graded check-in.
